_meta.notes: Chapter 1 Lesson 2. Previously taught: kumusta, mabuti, ikaw.

cards.body: Filipinos greet differently depending on the time of day. And every greeting starts with beauty.
Magandang means 'beautiful.' Filipino time-of-day greetings all start with this word.

example.note: Add the time of day after it.

cards.body: Umaga means 'morning.' Magandang umaga = beautiful morning = good morning.

cards.source_text: It's 8am. You walk into the office.
cards.prompt: How do you greet your coworker?
cards.explanation: Magandang umaga! = Good morning. Magandang always comes first.

cards.body: Gabi means 'evening' or 'night.' Same pattern: magandang gabi.

cards.source_text: It's 9pm. You arrive at a dinner party.
cards.prompt: How do you greet the host?
cards.explanation: Magandang gabi! = Good evening. Gabi is for evening and night.

cards.body: You can add kumusta after the time greeting for a longer, warmer hello.

cards.explanation: Umaga = morning. Magandang umaga = good morning.

cards.prompt: It's evening. Greet your friend, then ask how they are.
cards.explanation: Magandang gabi! Kumusta? = Good evening! How are you?

wrong_answer_tracking.behaviour: Wrong answers go to back of current session queue. User must get ALL questions right to complete. Cannot finish lesson with outstanding wrong answers.
wrong_answer_tracking.wrong_count_usage: Feed to Gemini system prompt for AI Practice.

alam_mo_ba: Magandang literally means 'beautiful.' Filipinos start every time-of-day greeting by calling that time beautiful.
There's also 'magandang hapon' (good afternoon) and 'magandang tanghali' (good noon). You'll learn those later.
In casual texts, some Filipinos shorten it to just 'morning!' or 'evening!' in Taglish.
